Okay, so it is 8 o'clock. So session number two. I would argue that today's session will be the most important session of everything that we do in copy camp. Ryan's going to leave the entire session. We'll kind of be our guest star here. We'll get to cable it a little bit later. But first, housekeeping notes. So everybody find a copy buddy. I need a copy buddy. Uh oh. Okay, so Matt needs one. You guys couldn't- Yeah, I need Pete now. Look at that team. Of course you need Ryan. Wait, how did this ruin- I asked if I emailed Ryan last night. But he would do it. I just got his email now. It's like, no, sorry, I'm thinking. That's like an all-starter rude. It's like asking to the prom or something. Matt's sloppy seconds here. It's CopyBuddy choice. Okay, so again, importance of copy buddy. You should be pitching your copy buddy every single day. You should hold each other accountable. If you did not put an idea in the idea sheet, very, very, very, very bad, hold your copy buddy accountable for that. You know, read promos together, compare notes together, stuff like that, right? Pitch each other, show each other headlines, show each other leads. That's the importance of the copy buddy. Again, write down an idea a day. I'm going to go over the three minimum requirements. Write down an idea a day. Read a promotion a day like we were just talking about. Write a page a day. Okay, so coming back to today. Again, today is going to be the most important session because it's about finding big ideas and it's about researching a big idea and making sure it's a big enough idea to go ahead and spend your time writing a promotion one. Nothing else will matter unless your idea is a good, good idea. You could be the best writer in the world, but if you're working with a bad idea, you're never gonna make that idea work. Now, conversely, you could be a horrific writer. You could be a horrific marketer, but if you come up with a brilliant breakthrough idea, it's hard to screw up. So for me, you guys have probably heard me talk about this. This is the importance of having what I call an unfuckable idea. I don't trust myself as a writer enough to write up a really hard idea. I have to make sure that the idea is unfuckable before I begin because I know I'm probably gonna try to fuck it up when I write it because I'm not a good enough writer. So I try to make sure that my idea is absolutely killer before I put pen to paper. Now, if you guys read that expectation sheet, you know that as copywriters for AF, we expect all of us write six promotions a year. So that's two months that you have to spend on every single promotion. So that's where the quote behind Ryan comes in. It's attributed to Abe Lincoln, but if you search around the internet, you'll find that he may or may not have said this, it may be attributed to someone else, but if you look at it, most people say Abe Lincoln said it, and there's a variation of this, a million variations in line, but generally it says give me five hours to chop down a tree and I'll spend the first four and a half sharpening the ax. That's the way I feel about copy too. So you have to write a promotion every eight weeks, every two months. If I were doing that, I'd spend six weeks trying to find my idea, six weeks without ever putting pen to paper, six weeks without ever taking that ax and hitting that tree. I'd be sharpening my idea for six weeks. So that's super strange, right? Because most people think as copywriters that you gotta start putting pen to paper day number one. I think that is a critical mistake. Yes, you should be writing every day, but I'd be abandoning a lot of ideas to try to find that one good sharpened idea before I went and spent the rest of the time writing it up. Because the writing part to me is the quick part. The hard part, the part of resharpening the ax all the time is finding that big idea. So I'm turning it over to Ryan. Ryan's gonna go through the rest of it. He's got some handouts and stuff like that. And I'm gonna get Caleb a little bit later. So a big question that you're gonna wrestle with your entire copywriting career is, what is a big idea? What makes up a big idea? How do you define it? One definition I've heard is a big idea is an idea that is instantly comprehended as important, exciting, and beneficial. It also leads to an inevitable conclusion, a conclusion that makes it easy to sell your product. So kind of breaking that apart, it's instantaneously comprehensible. It's important, it's exciting, it's beneficial, and it leads to a sale. And that kind of sounds a little bit like a fortune cookie. So what I thought I'd do is maybe look at, sometimes to understand the concept, it's important to look at what it's not. So first hand out here. The records with the access, I'm like, yeah. So I got a couple of examples. I wanna show you guys of promos that we did that were extremely successful, because I think they had powerful big ideas, versus what they could have been where they would not have been successful. Meaning how we could have taken the exacts, the guts of that same promotion and made it not a big idea. So, the first example you can look at is a promotion that I wrote that's done about $30 million, called the Impact System. If you haven't read it, if you want a copy of it, follow up with me after this morning. But what this could have been is something like Jim Rickards introduces new options trading strategy to take advantage of currency fluctuations. That's really what the strategy is. I mean, that's basically a world out with the Impact System is. But I mean, has Jim Rickards, if we could use this headline, has Jim Rickards and Jim has a lot of authority status in our business. So if we introduce something new, people maybe would have bought it. But options trading strategy sounds really complicated and extremely risky and currency fluctuations. I don't know what the hell that is, but it freaks me out. So I think that would have failed. I mean, if you compare that to Jim Rickards Impact Systems, the only way we've ever found that could have allowed you to profit an extraordinary 1200% from currency wars using the same secrets developed the CIA predict terrorist attacks. Next example was one that Scott Bardelli wrote called Canadian Social Security, a front end promotion. You know, basically the idea was you too can make a steady dependable returns investing in blue chip stocks. Wow, that's exciting. I mean, that sounds really boring. Sounds like something I've heard a zillion times before, but essentially that is the secret of Canadian Social Security. I mean, he added a bunch of proof in there about how that really is the same strategy used by the Canadian pension plan. And he added other proof of a real life person using this to get $400, almost $5,000 a month. You know, the idea of piggybacking is really intriguing, I think, to people. So I mean, you can see how something like a blue chip investing strategy can be taken and turned into a big idea. Just to put a point on what I was saying earlier in the beat of horse, which I like to do. This is sharpening the ax, right? So what you guys can't see in here with this example is that Scott Bardelli probably went through 30 iterations of this headline before he decided on the one that is at the bottom of this thing. So Scott continued to sharpen that ax. He did not write the rest of this promotion. He stayed on this idea for about four weeks and every other day he was coming into my office going, what if I did it like this? What if I did it like this? What if I did it like this? He did not start writing to say, oh, I'm gonna write about dividends. He didn't do that. He knew he had an idea. He kept sharpening the idea, sharpening the idea, sharpening the idea. And what you see at the end is what he ended up with and it got, I don't know, 40,000 names or something. Is that right? And at Joe's point, that's why we're doing this session, I think, is it's really easy to look at the end product and be like, wow, an agency of security. How did he come up with that? And that's, you know, he really did go through a process to get there and that's why I wanted to show you what he started out with and what he ended up with because that's really the whole trick. And it's hard when you look at a bunch of good ideas. It's important to do that so you understand them but I think it's also important to realize that that didn't just follow the sky for the most part. Okay, so we'll give the last example and we'll start it out with how to make money selling naked foot options. I have no idea what a naked foot option is. It sounds kind of, the naked part's kind of weird and I don't understand even what that means. So I mean, that became something big like, watch as we show three complete strangers how to instantly generate $127 cash in under three minutes without trading a single share stock without buying an option without ever even touching a mutual fund. And that's definitely a big idea to see somebody doing this live, outrageous social experiment on the streets of Baltimore. Can I provide some color to that one? Yeah, definitely. Sorry. I know I can't help myself. That's a rhyme. I can't help myself. Team effort. Do you guys, everybody knew David Blaine is, right? So David Blaine just had a new special on TV the other night called Beyond Magic. And you know, David Blaine goes out on the street, right? And he shows people car tricks out on the street and then they just go absolutely ballistic when the reveal of the trick comes out. And they start screaming and they're like, ah, fuck me, that's unbelievable, right? So when I was watching that, I'm like, this is where I came up with the idea for this promo. I'm like, man, I want to go out there and demonstrate doing this on the street with people. And I want to show that they could instantly generate money, even if they don't know anything about the stock market. I wanted them, I didn't get the same reaction, just David Blaine. But that was where the idea came from. I wanted them to have the exact same reaction as David Blaine had with these people when he revealed the magic trick. So my goal in doing this was to pay these people out and showing them how easy the strategy was. And I wanted them to be like, oh, my fucking God, I can't believe I just generated 300 fucking dollars in 30 seconds. Best I got was some woman saying, I couldn't believe I made 300 bucks on my way to Burger King. Still like decent. Not exactly what I wanted. But you can see how random things in your life can generate ideas here, right? Like that idea didn't come from me reading a textbook. That idea came from me being super lazy, sitting in bed one night and being like, fuck David Blaine, man, that's amazing. I want to do that to people too. How do I do that? And I don't know magic. So that's where the idea came from. But it was that constant sharpening of the ax again. Yeah, and that's, well, the next session I believe is how to generate those ideas, some ideas, and how to, a technique for producing ideas. But we put this session first because you have to know what a good idea even is before you look for one. So what are some other elements of a big idea? It's one great idea. It's not a mix of mediocre ones. Sometimes you may have heard around here the rule of one. It's just one thing. It's not a mix of boring things. It should just one big idea. A big believable promise of transformation, results, and outcome. It has to have some sort of promise in there that it's gonna benefit the person looking at it, their life in a way they can instantly understand. It's unique, original, unexpected, different from what the prospect has seen before. Any way you can make the idea seem new, that makes, can make it big. It's specific, concrete, tangible. It's not abstract or confusing. It's arresting, startling. It needs to shock them out of complacency. It should be easy to grasp with instant appeal. Somehow it should help them avoid the curse of knowledge. I don't know if you've ever read the book. What was that book called? No, I don't know. But simplicity encourages belief. Made to stick. Made to stick. That explains the curse of knowledge. Big ideas exist in a context and a place and time. That means it makes them very hard to steal. You can't just take somebody else's big idea from another business or from another time period and make it work today. They exist from here and now. It's worth studying past big ideas, but just because they work in the past doesn't mean they're necessarily big today. A big idea vibrates or glows when you see it. You'll instantly feel it in your gut. It should trigger the proverbial light bulb going off in your brain when you see it. And it should have the same effect in other people. Typically when you do come up with a big idea and you tell somebody else about it, and that's why we have the idea of copy buddies and fishing, you can instantly, hastily tell if that person gets it. And that's why I encourage, if you do think you have a big idea, you need to tell as many people as you can in person because you can get those non-verbal cues that you can't get over email or over Skype, whatever. I mean, if you just tell somebody face to face, right away you can see their pupils open and you can just tell non-verbally that they're very excited about it. If you don't get that, that probably means you really don't have a big idea. Big ideas don't necessarily have to be about something big in the world, but they do have to be about something big in the reader's life. The countless promos we've written about tiny obscure unknown investments that could make the reader rich. And you have to tell in this business that the idea of getting rich quick is something that's a big idea to most people. However they can do that. So if you look at the next handout and go to the bottom circles, that kind of explains that point a little better. You see these three concentric circles. The one that says like big news, coming event, prediction, that's usually what a lot of people talk about are a big idea. For example, someone might think like 3D printing is a big idea and that's part of it. Or like a dollar collapse of being a big idea. That could, those are things in the world that are certainly big and they can be made be a big idea in promotion, but that alone isn't enough. It has to somehow intersect with the prospect desires or fears. I mean just because something's big in the world doesn't mean our readers really care about it. And it has to somehow intersect with what we're selling. The newsletter or service or something that relates to why they need to buy it, whatever we're selling. And looking at the top graph, you'll see kind of a graph there. Another way of looking at what a big idea is. I got this from John Carlton actually. And you can look on the corner there where it says cold hard reality and you can look at the appeal for most people and almost nobody cares but it isn't really, cold hard reality doesn't really appeal to anybody and they have enough of that in their own life. If you look on going out on the access to magic, that's what people really want. And that appeals to everybody. Everybody wants a magic solution when they buy something. That's why we buy things from something one day like a new kind of food we want to eat all the way up to some sort of stock trading system. We want magic. We don't really have magic. So the trick is how do we give them something that feels like magic that's real? Can I go for a second? Okay, so look at those bottom three concentric circles again. Ryan mentioned 3D printing, right? So most people think 3D printing at that time was a big idea. We wrote a 3D printing promo that did probably 15,000 names, maybe 20,000 names, but the headline on our 3D printing promo kind of fit that middle spot there, which it wasn't just about 3D printing. The headline of our 3D printing promo was click print gun. So it kind of got into the mainstream, which was this gun control debate that was going on. So there you had the prospect's desires or fears mixed with the big news that's coming about 3D printing. So of course the promotion led with this idea that there is a new technology that basically makes gun control the most silly debate on the planet because there's new technology that you could click and print your own gun. And it's this new technology that's gonna make a lot of people rich because it's not just guns that you can click and print. You can click and print almost anything. So look at the rise of these 3D printing companies, yada yada yada. So that was one way that we took 3D printing, which was a normal idea and put it in the middle of that concentric circle by attaching it to something that was the prospect's desire or fear. Same thing, same franchise, but different idea was virtual reality. There's an errands franchise. So virtual reality, again, I mean virtual reality's been talked about for a long time, specifically in the past like a year, let's say it's been heating up. So how do you take virtual reality and put it in the middle of that prospect's desires or fears? Well, the way that Aaron did it was we focused on the foreign industry, which was the headline was not safe for work. And we developed this desire and intrigue about a video clip that was gonna prove a new technology that was about to change the world, but oh, don't watch it at work because it's gonna be a pretty racy, raunchy video clip. So does that make sense to everybody? Like how do you take that idea that might just be about 3D printing? That might just be about virtual reality and move it to that inside of that circle by attaching it to the prospect's desires and fears? Does anyone have any questions about what a big idea it is before we move on to a tool that can help you adapt a final one? Is that concept pretty clear? So, I spent five years trying to figure out what a big idea it is. And what I did recently is I tried to put everything together into basically a checklist of all the parts and pieces of what I think make up a big idea. And I put it together in this template that you guys are getting right now. If you guys want a digital copy of this, just hit me up after the class today. And a big disclaimer on this is like, this tool isn't gonna automatically create a big idea for you just because you fill in the blanks. It's not gonna just magically pop out. But I think it's gonna help you guys identify the pieces and parts that go into what a big idea is and just the process of trying to fill in all these blanks. I'm pretty confident that a true big idea is just gonna pop in your head by doing that. And once you have an idea, you can double check it on this. We're looking at the template. I'm just gonna quickly go through all, what all these mean. I mean, some of it's pretty self-explanatory, but you wanna put the product and editor in there. I would say like, one important thing about this is like, if you're starting out and you see blanks and you don't know, that's good. That just shows you the opportunity. For example, you don't need to fill this thing out sequentially, starting at the very top and just bam, bam, bam, bam. Like, start out, whatever you have, just put it in here. If you only have an investment strategy, put that in there. If you only have the product, put that in here. If you only have some sort of a headline or hope, put that in there to start off with. Because the process of filling all these blanks in, that's where the opportunity is. Now, I'm gonna be a quick story is we had an idea of, Joe and I came up with this idea of the magic calendar, a sort of calendar that would tell you the exact dates to get rich in the stock market. That actually involved trading around what are called peduva dates, which are dates that the FDA releases, where they basically promise a company that they're gonna have approved their drug by a certain dates. And what I found from my research was that stocks tend to just go up ahead of those dates. So if you knew the dates in advance, then you get in and you can ride that up in a lot of cases. We didn't have a product for that. We couldn't find a product that fit it. So in that case of how to use this template, that would have been a big blank. What we ended up doing is hiring the editor and creating a service then to fit that idea. So if you have a blank, that's the opportunity for you to creatively figure out how do you fill it in. So, and you don't have to start at the top. These are all just kind of in order that seem to make sense to me. But so that's how I would use it. I mean, I would go through, you can collect your answers any way you want. You don't have to fill in these little lines to your pen and ink. I've used no cards in the past. I've put it out of a page for each answer. You can fill it in as little or as many words as you want, it makes sense to you. Again, if you have any question, there's no fixed answer. And, you know, some notes on some of these things. But the actual money making opportunity, I think that was really important. I think a lot of people have ideas that are kind of half baked, because they don't know what they're actually selling. You know, my advice in this would be take all the copywriting out of it, take all the salesmanship out of it, and just list explicitly and simplistically what you are selling, what the idea is, for example, and I'll show you another example using my impact system, but for the magic calendar, I would have said, it's trading around for newfoundates, buying them when the date is released, the pressure leaves, and selling them after the, or before the actual date assigned by the FDA. You know, if it's a stock, put the stock in there in the ticker symbol, and not just that, but also put it in like the strategy. You know, the strategy is to buy the stock now, nobody sells it, you know, no one knows about it, and then sell it after this certain event happens. You know, I think you gotta have proof. In starting out, you have to do some research to find out that you have some sort of proof. I mean, later you might find more proof that's better, but the magic mechanism is just kind of a phrase I've heard developed by another operator. That's the actual thing that makes your product work. So every product that we're selling and copy has the sort of thing that makes it work. It could be a strategy, it could be, if it's a single stock tease, it could be a new technology being developed, but whatever it is that's gonna help them achieve, what they want them to achieve with the promises, that's the magic mechanism. A lot of the stuff that makes sense when I go through a real example, but does anyone have any questions before I give you guys a real live example of how this works? You guys may, again, I'm gonna keep coming back to this point, right? Six promos a year, that's it. Six promos a year, that's what we expect. So you may fill this out 15 times before you decide on an idea, and that's totally fine. Like you may start filling this out and be like, damn, I just can't figure out how to do this, or I can't figure out how to do that. So in the course of an eighth week cycle producing a promotion, you can fill this out multiple times on multiple different ideas, that is totally fine. There's a process that we're gonna talk about later that's an influence process called commitment and consistency, and it's this idea that we as human beings, when we commit to a process, we wanna be consistent with that process. That can destroy you as a copywriter though. So, you know, oftentimes we commit to an idea, and we're like, okay, that's my idea, I'm gonna make it work. And then you hit these roadblocks, and you're like, damn, I'm still gonna make that idea work. There are certain times, we talked about this last week, right, that you have to disconnect yourself from commitment and consistency as a copywriter, just ditch the idea. So like, because you've started to write things down, don't feel like you have to commit to making that your idea that you're gonna write off during that eight week cycle. Fill this out multiple times. You're gonna try multiple different ideas. That's why it's so important to write down an idea every single day, because if you commit to only one idea in an eight week cycle, that's like having one bullet to try to hit the target. You gotta have multiple, multiple rounds in your arsenal to try to hit that target. So this is where an idea a day comes into play, because there are a lot of ideas that are just never gonna fit into this template. You're gonna find out right away. So keep trying, keep trying. You know, and also as you study promos every day, you can kind of reverse engineer, as you can see how I did in a minute, one promo thorough, but you could, you know, right away, look at a promo you're studying and be like, why is this unique? You know, how do the, how do the copywriter portray this is the most important thing in our lifetimes? And just see how many blanks you can fill in. And I'm pretty sure, I'm very confident that in most cases, you're gonna find a way to fill in every single blank if you're looking at successful promos. Looking at page two, there's a bunch of other formulas that might help you understand what a big idea is. I kind of consider it like, these are like my top filters that I'll run an idea through. A few questions on these, just seek me out after the class and I kind of explain them. Another thing too, one dimension was that this template is very much designed for opportunity based promotions, specifically how to make money in the investment markets. I think it could be easily adapted though, to whatever you're selling. For example, if you're selling some sort of the Lumen Doom prediction, you just have to change a couple of words in this template and this still would work. For example, if it was like a Lumen Doom promotion about a dollar crash, you wouldn't say, you'd want to know the actual money making opportunity, but just based on that kind of format or genre of promo, you'd really want to be explicitly, what is going to create doomsday and as explicitly as possible. You know, it's like, what were the magic mechanisms? I mean, call them all, instead of magic mechanisms, call them like the doomsday device that's going to blow up the world. A lot of these things could be adapted for health or products, whatever, you're just a little bit of modification. I don't know how many guys have actually gone through the promo that I wrote called the impact system. If you haven't, then some of this isn't going to make a lot of sense, but I'm still going to go through it. Product and editor was Jim Rickard's currency wars. It was a brand new product. Pete and I had went up in Anderson's office and talked about it and basically just explained to me how it worked and that's where I kind of got the strategy, the actual money making opportunity was buying puts on calls on ETFs and stocks that are moving because of what Jim considered currency wars. That's really it. I looked at the best qualities of Jim though. I mean, his CIA background, he literally wrote the book on currency wars and I knew like, Jim was going to be a big part of what made the impact system work. Looking at the proof I had, I mean, I knew I needed to find at least 25 different back-tested examples of ways you could have made 1,000%. I think the first one I found was an ETF track in gold called GLD and a few times, like during the 2008 crisis that had a few of them in at the right time and out at the right time, you could have made as much as like 8,000%. So I knew there was some sort of proof in there. And then I got a lot of the proof from his books. I mean, there's a story in a book how he helped the CIA detect unusual options trades around the 9-11 attacks and then a financial war game that he had been a part of. I think it satisfies the primal desire to make a lot of money fast. But you can see how I tried to make it a lot more explicit and get the 1,000% promise in there. And that kind of, having all those pieces and parts is what kind of had me come up with was just the idea of the impact system. I knew I wanted to like bundle all those together into one thing, some sort of systematic way you could play currency war to make money. And to be honest, the idea just kind of popped in my head. I'll go over more next week, but it really was from the book, Technique for Producing Ideas, where I talked to Pete, I'd done a lot of thought on it. And basically what happened was a couple days after that, I was going to bed and just laying in my bed it was probably like 11 o'clock. And the word impact just popped in my brain. This was sort of accurate. And I emailed it to myself and my phone. And I only knew a couple of the words. I was like international monetary, something, something, currency trading. And the next day I kind of figured out what the rest of the blanks were. But I tried to simulate as much as possible that whole process in this document of what I went through. Does anyone else have any questions? You can go over this on your own time actually, going through all these different plans. Ryan's too humble to say this, but just to kind of put this in context, that promo was the biggest backend promo we've ever had in Agora Financial. I think if you look at overall Agora, it was top three in overall backend revenue in all of Agora. So that promo, not just number one in Agora Financial, but my guess is it's gonna end up somewhere either two or three in all of Agora. So if you guys haven't read it, you should read it, read it, read it, read it. There's two promos that I would read at least once a year. One would be End of America, which was the largest front end promotion ever written in Agora history. And the second would be Impact, because it's one of the largest backend promos ever written in Agora history. You know, people often say, right, that there are certain books that they reread every year. I reread that promo every year, both those promos are here. Does anyone else have any, does anyone have any questions at all on the template? There's a lot of stuff here I know, but, I mean, what are your initial thoughts on it? Does anyone have any initial thoughts? Do you think it's gonna be useful? Do you think it seems too complicated? Somebody's got to talk. I'm gonna call a few people. I like that you put the example of what you would do. I think it's helpful to like, when you showed it to me before, there were a couple things I was like, I'm clear on, but I think it's kind of really clear. Yeah, and you know, I want to make it clear, like this is my own personal template. You guys certainly, and I've stolen all these pieces of parts from lots of different other templates, and none of these ideas I actually developed my own. And I took these all from more brilliant copywriters than I ever will be. So you could definitely take this template and make your own version of it, just by putting in the pieces of parts that you think make sense. I mean, I guess why I call it Ryan's big idea template, but you know, if someday you create Rich's own template, that's really what you should do, is create your own tools like these ultimately. That helps us start with something. Now as you're doing something like this, you try to stay as large of an idea as you can, or do you, as smaller, more like smaller ideas come in about that, are you focusing on those when they kind of come to you, or are you kind of just more just saying, let me get the big, I need to bubble down and I'll get to those, when I get to those? I would say, and again, like this is more of a stimulation of how it goes. Like really my core idea was the impact system, and it just made sense in my head what that was by going through this process. That should in itself generate all the blanks, if it's a powerful idea. In fact, it should generate then 30 pages of copy without much trouble. If you're getting to the point where you think you have an idea and you can't fill in these blanks, it's gonna be very difficult then to fill in, I think, you know, another 20 to 30 pages of copy to sell it. So I think a big idea just naturally creates a lot more small ideas that complement it, that prove it, that can help close the sale. And in later sessions, Joe's gonna show us a technique called copy painting that is a way to very quickly generate whatever else you need to make this work. But I would say generally all the things you'll see in this template too are in the lead. So if you can fill this in, you should at least be able to get five pages of copy, and then using the technique that Joe will show you later, the copyboarding, we just list all the objections and how to answer them, it should be pretty easy then to get the rest of the package together. I would say that it only took me about a week and a half to write the impact system to actually write it. That probably went two or three or four drafts of it. It took me a whole month to look up all the examples and do all the research. And it took a couple of weeks to figure out the idea itself. But once you have the idea and once you have the proof, it does not take long to actually write it. I didn't do a lot of writing because I already understood enough of the idea and I showed it to other people. If you go through my office, you can see, what was the total on that receipt? I was like, 36 bucks or something. Yeah, I took Joe and Pete out to lunch and pitched him on the idea. And I could instantaneously just within five seconds tell that I hit some sort of nerve because I could just look at them and tell if they got right away the huge potential opportunity of what the idea had. And I think we figured out their return on that 36 dollar lunch was like, I can't do that. 13,000% return. 13,000, right? So it's like, 100,000, 100,000% return. You know, I have to tell you, it's a very exciting feeling when it happens. When you come up with a big idea and tell other people and you can tell it really as big. It's also a huge burden because that was like, when I had to actually pull this thing off and if it bombs, it's on me. So that's how it goes. That's how the, you know, that's how every promotion goes when you write it. So let's talk about that exciting feeling and real quick, just for a second. So coming back to the topic, here comes the big damning admission from my side. Who in this room watches the bachelor? No one? I've seen it before. Oh, okay. So Sonia and I. I've seen it. I've seen it. And Erica, I've watched the bachelor. So I'm gonna set it up for you guys that don't know this, right? You take one dude and you put 30 women in the room who all wanna marry this dude. And each week, and they all live in a house together. All the women live in a house together. And each week he's gotta vote these women out of the house until he finds, you know, his soulmate. And what happens is that there's inevitably one asshole there all the time. And in this case, let's say the asshole is a female, right? So this asshole female will not get along with anybody else in the house. And she will piss everybody else off. And then they put the camera on there and they're basically like, why are you being an asshole? And she always says, now there's 21 seasons of the bachelor. There's always this asshole. And this asshole will always say, I'm not here to make friends. I'm only here for the guy, right? You as a copy buddy. It's not a copy friend. You are not here to make friends. You're here to be brutally honest with your copy buddy. Do not think about it as making a friend. When your copy buddy pitches you an idea, don't be afraid to be like, don't be afraid to be the asshole. And be like, that idea is fucking terrible. That idea is terrible. Don't waste your time on it, right? Don't be the nice guy all the time to your copy buddy. Be honest. Because it's that honesty that's gonna make that copywriter a better copywriter. It's gonna be that honesty that has them not writing up a shitty idea over the course of their eight weeks, right? So anytime your copy buddy pitches you an idea, think back to the bachelor house. And think back to, I'm not here to make friends. I'm here to be honest about what's happening in this idea. And again, you're gonna know it instantly, right? You're gonna pitch a copy by then. They're gonna be like, if they're a nice person and they don't wanna be the asshole, you're gonna pitch a copy by then they're gonna go, okay, like that's it, right? Because they don't wanna be an asshole. They just kinda wanna be nice. You should be the asshole. Be like, that fucking idea sucks. Please don't do that idea. That's a terrible idea. You know, I think if you tell them a good idea, they're gonna be like, oh yeah, what if you did it this way? What if you did it that way, right? The hell's hitting me in the head, Harry? Don't worry about it. Don't worry about it. It's been so exciting. It's been so exciting. I didn't know if that was like something to do with the bachelor that was hitting me in the head or what. You know what I'm saying? But don't be afraid to be the asshole. You're gonna see it on your copy buddy's face, right? Again, not a copy friend. You're not here to be a copy friend. You're here to make friends. You're here to make you better, your copy thetter, your copy buddy's copy thetter. And you could use this template I gave you to ask your questions to your copy buddy too. So they pitch you an idea. You can say, well, how does that satisfy the primal desire of somebody to get rich? If they can't answer that, that's probably a problem if you're doing an investment-based newsletter. You know, what is the magic mechanism? So one thing I wanna note on that magic mechanism I just thought of is that, and I used to be confused by this point, you can have more than one. For example, the magic mechanisms that make the impact system work, they're currency wars. What's a currency war? I don't know, you need to read the book on it, right? I mean, I pretty much just assume the reader knows that the currency wars are something that somehow move the currency markets. But I don't really explain in the copy what it is. So I mean, you can have more than one thing that moves within the product itself to let the person achieve the desires they want. So that's why I put two in here. You can have, I don't know, you can have multiple different mechanisms that make it work, probably fewer, better than more. Is there any other questions you guys have about this template or big ideas in general? Do you find that you mostly, because in your two examples, you know the thing that we're, you're not supposed to try to find a market for your product, you want to find a product to fit your market. But in your two examples, it's a kind of almost opposite, where Joe had this idea of, what's his name, David Blaine, right? And then he said, man, I want to sell that process, and now I gotta find a product to fit that process, right? So we went out and found that. But yours was kind of almost the opposite. You said, this currency war thing, it's pretty cool. I want to find it. I mean, you didn't need to search for a market, but you kind of, in essence kind of did, because you said, you know, I need to find, I have this product, now I need to find a way to, how do I get it to these guys, so that they, you know, fitting all these criteria. I would say, I mean, do you find that you, more often, you're just, you know, watching David Blaine, or reading George Report, and then try to say, hey, how do I fit that shit to one of these? Or do you say, hey man, this is a cool system, I'm gonna find a way to pin fit to the masses? I would say that probably both happened. That's why, yeah, I know. I wish I could give you an easier answer. Sometimes I start with the product itself, like we have a launch or something, then you kind of, somebody has to sell it.
